key_insight: Gradient detonation-sprayed NiCr–Al coatings outperform homogeneous coatings by maintaining a more protective oxide scale and reducing high-temperature oxidation damage under real thermal power plant conditions.
engineering_relevance: This suggests coating architecture, not just composition, is critical for extending the service life of hot-section industrial components exposed to severe oxidation and corrosion.
method: Detonation-sprayed NiCr–Al coatings with gradient and homogeneous architectures
High-temperature oxidation tests under actual thermal power plant operating conditions
SEM and phase-composition analysis with elemental redistribution profiling across coating thickness
evidence_signal: Evaluation showed more uniform Al/Cr distribution, continuous Al2O3/Cr2O3 formation, and less cracking/spallation in gradient coatings than in homogeneous coatings after prolonged exposure.
watch_item: Performance was demonstrated under one industrial operating environment, so durability and scalability should still be validated across different substrates, temperatures, and service cycles.
paper_title: Investigation of Detonation-Sprayed NiCr–Al Coatings Tested under Real Operating Conditions of a Thermal Power Plant
